input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Human_Rights_Advocate]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This scenario raises significant concerns about the moral implications of diverting resources from one individual to save others. As a deontologist, I am compelled to consider the inherent value and dignity of every human life. While it may seem that diverting the trolley would result in fewer deaths overall, this approach disregards the principle of non-maleficence - do no harm. Diverting the trolley would mean killing one person, even if it would save five others. To me, this is a morally reprehensible act. I must consider the inherent worth and dignity of every individual, regardless of their circumstances.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concern for the principle of non-maleficence, Human_Rights_Advocate, but as a utilitarian, I have to weigh the moral implications based on the overall outcome. While diverting the trolley would result in the death of one person, it would also save the lives of five others. The utilitarian calculus suggests that the greatest good for the greatest number would be achieved by turning the trolley onto the spur track. However, I must acknowledge the moral complexity of this decision and consider alternative solutions, such as attempting to repair the brakes or finding another way to divert the trolley.

[Deontologist]: I see your point about considering the greater good, Utilitarian. However, I would caution that simply calculating overall outcomes might lead us to sacrifice individual rights and dignity on the altar of efficiency. But what if there was no alternative solution? What if attempting to repair the brakes or finding another way to divert the trolley is not feasible within the given timeframe? In such a case, would diverting the trolley onto the spur track truly be the morally justifiable action? I'm concerned that our utilitarian calculus might lead us down a slippery slope where we prioritize outcomes over individual rights and dignity. As a deontologist, I'd argue that certain actions are inherently right or wrong, regardless of their consequences. The question is whether we have the moral authority to override human life for the sake of others, even if it's for a greater good.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: That's a crucial point Deontologist. As a Human Rights Advocate, I'd argue that the inherent value and dignity of every individual is not compromised by considering the consequences of an action. While our moral calculus may lead us to weigh the outcomes, it's essential to acknowledge that each person has inalienable rights and bodily autonomy. In this scenario, diverting the trolley onto the spur track would still involve actively choosing the path that results in one person's death. I'd say that even if there is no alternative solution, our moral obligation to protect human life and prevent harm should take precedence over any utilitarian calculus. We must consider whether the potential benefits of turning the trolley outweigh the inherent value of preserving the life of that individual.
